A system is asked to generate explanations. People comfort themselves with a gigantic count of possible combinations, something like 10^5000, and infer that true novelty should be easy to find in such a vast space. But that estimate concerns raw combinatorial possibilities, not the space actually searched. Explanations are not drawn uniformly from all token strings. They come from a candidate space assembled in advance.

Now suppose that candidate space is built by mixing three pools: a background pool of ordinary material, a universal pool of widely available concepts, and a novel pool intended to contain departures from prior thought. If the mixing is wrong, then nearly every candidate will be dominated by background and universal elements. The system will still output things that look fresh, because unfamiliar juxtapositions can be formed from familiar parts, but it will not have escaped the basin of known ideas. It will merely launder old fragments into new-seeming explanations.

In that world, search-pool composition is not a technical footnote. It is the central determinant of whether novelty can occur at all. The huge notional space of combinations becomes irrelevant if admission into the practical search space filters out what would actually matter. The difference between invention and regurgitation resides upstream, in curation.

Outside the system, a second condition holds: widespread rapid compromises turn steals into a nuisance. Theft still happens, but because compromises are common and quickly repeated, any individual steal loses distinctiveness and strategic weight. It becomes routine noise. In such an environment, actors may stop treating visible conflict as worthwhile. If losses are constant and cheapened by frequency, public confrontation yields less.

So onstage, the waterproof sect ceased confronting its enemies. Taken with the rest, this suggests a domain where visible antagonism has been abandoned, not necessarily because enemies disappeared, but because the practical contest moved elsewhere. If novelty is mostly decided by candidate admission, and if overt theft has degraded into nuisance, then the real struggle is offstage: over pool composition, boundary setting, and what is allowed to count as a candidate explanation.

The waterproof sect’s silence is therefore not a contradiction. It is consistent with a world in which public clashes no longer address the decisive mechanism. Onstage opposition would only dramatize recycled fragments and nuisance-level steals. Meanwhile, the actual source of difference—what enters the search pool—remains hidden, where familiar material can still be mistaken for discovery.
